sourceMovie.title: Constantine
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Constantine works as a fantasy movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The fantasy and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Constantine: City of Demons - The Movie
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Constantine's fantasy stayed with you, Constantine: City of Demons - The Movie (2018) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The action through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Constantine excels in fantasy, Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness (2022) doubles down on action to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Nimona
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Nimona (2023) is the natural next step after Constantine: same fantasy DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and action elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Avengers: Infinity War
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Constantine who respond to its action will find Avengers: Infinity War (2018) immediately familiar. Both films share a adventure sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Howl's Moving Castle
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Constantine fans keep landing on Howl's Moving Castle (2004) — the fantasy is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Beetlejuice
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Beetlejuice draws from the same fantasy well as Constantine, channeling comedy in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1988 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Insidious
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Insidious (2011) captures the horror appeal of Constantine without simply imitating it. The thriller streak running through both films is what puts Insidious at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Black Adam
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The action appeal of Constantine — that tense edge, the adventure — translates cleanly into Black Adam (2022). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Insidious: Chapter 3
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Insidious: Chapter 3 (2015) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps Constantine rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Thunderbolts*
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Thunderbolts* (2025) is the kind of film Constantine fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The action overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

faq.question: What should I watch after Constantine?
faq.answer: Try Constantine: City of Demons - The Movie, Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness, Nimona—each shares DNA with Constantine while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Constantine?
faq.answer: Constantine (2005) is best described as Fantasy, Action, Horror. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Constantine part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Constantine Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
